stem:
A 22-month-old boy is brought to his GP because of pallor. He was born at 39 weeks (3.4 kg) and was breastfed until 11 months, transitioning to full-cream cow's milk. He drinks about 900 mL of cow's milk per day from a bottle, including overnight, and refuses red meat, eggs and most green vegetables. He passes soft stool daily with no blood, has had no recurrent infections, no bruising and no overseas travel.

He is developmentally appropriate (walked 13 months, about 20 words). Examination: alert, well, height and weight on the 50th centile, conjunctival pallor, no jaundice, no organomegaly, no purpura. HR 118, RR 28, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent on room air. Bloods: Hb 80 g/L, MCV 62, MCH 19, RDW 18 percent, platelets 480, WCC 8.2, ferritin 6, CRP under 5, reticulocytes 1.0 percent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118 bpm, RR 28, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, soft non-tender abdomen, no organomegaly, no purpura.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 80 g/L, MCV 62 fL, MCH 19 pg, RDW 18%, platelets 480 x10^9/L, WCC 8.2 x10^9/L, ferritin 6 microg/L, CRP <5 mg/L, reticulocytes 1.0%.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day, increase iron-rich foods AND commence oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day; recheck Hb at 4 weeks (expect a rise of at least 10 g/L)
options.rationale: Iron deficiency anaemia from excess cow's milk: low ferritin, microcytic anaemia with high RDW and mild thrombocytosis. RCH CPG guidance: dietary modification (cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day) PLUS oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day, with a 4-week response check, continuing for 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores.

options.text: Refer for inpatient packed red cell transfusion now because Hb is under 90 g/L, with type-and-screen and consent for transfusion at the receiving hospital
options.rationale: Haemodynamically stable and asymptomatic; transfusion reserved for symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 with CV compromise. Would be correct with symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 (Source: RCH CPG: Anaemia).

options.text: Order haemoglobin electrophoresis and HbA2 quantification before any treatment to exclude underlying thalassaemia trait or variant disease
options.rationale: Ferritin unequivocally low and dietary history fits iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screening is only considered after iron therapy failure or by ethnic background. Would be correct after failed iron replacement (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Day-stay IV iron polymaltose infusion now, bypassing any trial of oral iron in this otherwise well child with chronic dietary deficiency aetiology
options.rationale: IV iron in a toddler is reserved for malabsorption, oral intolerance or non-response to oral therapy. Would be correct only after oral trial failure (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: Multivitamin containing 5 mg elemental iron daily and continue current 1.5 L/day milk intake without specific dietary modification or recheck
options.rationale: Multivitamin iron (5 mg) is inadequate (required 3-6 mg/kg/day, ~36-72 mg for this 12 kg child) and excessive milk perpetuates deficiency. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency).

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia in toddlers is most commonly driven by excessive cow's milk intake (>500-600 mL/day), which displaces iron-rich solids and causes occult GI blood loss. Treatment is dietary modification plus oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day for 3 months total (1 month to correct Hb, then continue to refill stores).
key_points: Cow's milk cap 500 mL/day from 12 months
Oral iron 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental, once daily, with vitamin C source
Expect Hb rise of 10 g/L in 4 weeks
Treat for 3 months total to refill stores
Low ferritin + high RDW + microcytosis = iron deficiency until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Mild thrombocytosis is common in iron deficiency and is not lymphoproliferative; it resolves with iron repletion.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Iron deficiency
RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

subtopic_detail: Microcytic anaemia in a toddler with dietary risk factor

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP by her parents for ongoing management of atopic dermatitis diagnosed at 4 months. She has had multiple flares despite the parents using an over-the-counter aqueous cream as a soap substitute and 1% hydrocortisone applied 'when it gets really bad' for two to three days at a time, then stopped. She has dry skin with lichenified plaques in the antecubital and popliteal fossae, mild erythema on the cheeks, and excoriated patches on the dorsum of both hands.

Her sleep is disrupted by scratching most nights. She had cow's milk protein allergy as an infant which has resolved, and her father has hay fever. There is no fever, no weeping or honey-crusting, and her growth and development are normal. The parents are worried about 'steroid damage' and ask whether they can avoid topical steroids altogether.

data_table.Examination: Generalised xerosis. Lichenified flexural plaques. Excoriated dorsa of hands. No pustules, no monomorphic vesicles, no honey-crust. Weight on 60th centile.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate maintenance regimen?

options.text: Stop topical steroids; use thick paraffin-based emollient three to four times daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Emollients alone do not control established eczema with lichenification; appropriate TCS use is safe and effective. Would be correct only for very mild dry skin without inflammation (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema).

options.text: Thick emollient BD, methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment daily then twice weekly, hydrocortisone 1% face
options.rationale: Site-matched topical corticosteroid potency (mild for face, moderate such as methylprednisolone aceponate for trunk/flexures) plus generous emollient is standard. Once-daily TCS until clear and then twice-weekly proactive application reduces flares (RCH CPG Eczema; TG Dermatology).

options.text: Betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% twice daily to face and body until clear, then stop
options.rationale: Potent TCS should not be used on the face in young children; boom-bust without proactive maintenance leads to repeated flares. Would never be correct in this age group on face (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Refer for systemic ciclosporin given the chronicity of her disease today
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe refractory eczema after optimised topical therapy; she has not had adequate site-matched TCS plus emollient. Would be correct only after failed maximised topical regimen (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Daily bleach baths and oral cefalexin for two weeks as antimicrobial maintenance
options.rationale: Routine oral antibiotics do not improve non-infected eczema and drive resistance; bleach baths may help colonisation but are not maintenance monotherapy. Would be correct only for documented infected eczema (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Atopic dermatitis maintenance combines liberal emollients with site-matched topical corticosteroids (mild on face/genitals, moderate on body/limbs, potent only with specialist input on thickened plaques) and proactive twice-weekly application to flare-prone sites once skin is clear.
key_points: Emollient at least twice daily, including soap substitute
Face: hydrocortisone 1% (mild); flexures/body: methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% (moderate)
Apply once daily until clear, then twice weekly proactively
Wet wraps over TCS for severe flares (short course)
Food allergy testing only if reproducible food-triggered flare
explanation.pearls: Aqueous cream contains sodium lauryl sulphate and is no longer recommended as a leave-on emollient; it can drive flares.

stem:
A 3-year-old boy with longstanding moderate atopic dermatitis is brought to ED by his mother with a 36-hour history of a rapidly worsening rash on his face and neck, fever to 38.7 degC, and poor oral intake. He has been using methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment twice weekly proactively and an emollient four times daily, with good control until this week.

There has been no overseas travel and his immunisations are up to date including MMR and varicella. His maternal grandfather had a cold sore on his lip when he kissed the patient three days ago. On examination he is miserable, drinking small sips, with grouped 2-3 mm punched-out monomorphic erosions and umbilicated vesicles in clusters on the left cheek, periorbital region and chin, some now coalescing.

There is no honey-coloured crusting elsewhere, no ocular involvement on fluorescein staining, no neck stiffness, and no focal neurology. His pre-existing flexural eczema is otherwise stable. There are scattered satellite lesions on the right cheek and on his chest. He has had no contact with hand-foot-and-mouth disease at day care, no prior episodes of this kind, and his last wet nappy was 8 hours ago.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% on room air, capillary refill 2 sec.
data_table.Examination: Clusters of monomorphic umbilicated 2-3 mm vesicles and punched-out erosions on left cheek, periorbital and chin. No corneal fluorescein uptake. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside HSV PCR swab from a deroofed vesicle sent. FBC, CRP, U&E pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Daily bleach baths and increase topical methylprednisolone aceponate to twice daily on the face
options.rationale: Potent TCS on face with concurrent HSV is harmful; bleach baths do not treat HSV. Would never be correct in suspected eczema herpeticum (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for five days and continue topical corticosteroids as usual
options.rationale: This is HSV not impetigo; mupirocin does not treat the virus, and continuing TCS over HSV can worsen dissemination. Would be correct in confirmed impetigo (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral cefalexin four times daily for seven days for impetiginised eczema
options.rationale: Honey-crusting and polymorphic pustules would suggest S aureus impetigo; monomorphic umbilicated vesicles and HSV contact indicate eczema herpeticum. Would be correct with classic impetigo features (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Admit, commence IV aciclovir 8-hourly and arrange urgent ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, fever, an HSV contact and rapid progression in an atopic child are eczema herpeticum. Periorbital involvement risks HSV keratitis, mandating admission, IV aciclovir and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG Eczema herpeticum).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

options.text: Discharge home on oral valaciclovir with planned review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Periorbital involvement, fever and poor intake in a 3-year-old with eczema herpeticum warrant admission for IV therapy and ophthalmology. Would be correct only for mild non-facial eczema herpeticum with good intake (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

explanation.summary: Eczema herpeticum is disseminated HSV in atopic skin, presenting with monomorphic 2-3 mm umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, systemic features and an HSV contact. Periocular disease threatens the cornea and mandates IV aciclovir plus ophthalmology review.
key_points: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles = HSV (eczema herpeticum)
Polymorphic pustules with honey-crust = S aureus impetiginised eczema
Periocular HSV = admit, IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h, ophthalmology
Withhold TCS on actively infected sites until controlled
Co-infection with S aureus is common; cover if features suggest
explanation.pearls: If the morphology is monomorphic, think herpetic. If you see punched-out erosions in an atopic child, treat as HSV until proven otherwise.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

subtopic_detail: Eczema flare with painful clustered vesicles

stem:
A 7-week-old boy is brought to ED by his parents because he has not opened his bowels for 6 days and has had increasing abdominal distension and two episodes of bilious vomiting overnight. He was born at 39 weeks by normal vaginal delivery, weighing 3.5 kg. The mother reports that he did not pass meconium until day 3 of life and the midwife had needed to perform a rectal stimulation.

He is exclusively formula-fed at 90 mL every 3 hours but has been taking less today. His weight today is 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.5 kg). When the registrar performs a careful digital rectal examination there is an explosive release of foul gas and liquid stool. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. There is no fever. The anus is normally positioned, the sacrum is intact and lower limb neurology is normal.

The plain abdominal X-ray shows dilated bowel loops with a paucity of rectal gas. Antenatal scans were unremarkable and there was no polyhydramnios. The newborn screening heel-prick was reported as normal including immunoreactive trypsinogen. He has no associated dysmorphism, no abnormal facies and no cardiac murmur.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 98%, capillary refill 3 sec.
data_table.Examination: Distended abdomen, tympanic, ribbon-like rectum on PR, explosive release on PR. Anus normally sited. No sacral dimple. Weight gain inadequate (only 700 g over 7 weeks).
data_table.Investigations: AXR: dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas. FBC, U&E, VBG sent.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Functional constipation; commence oral macrogol disimpaction and discharge with GP review
options.rationale: Delayed meconium over 48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting and explosive PR are red flags for organic cause; discharge without further investigation is unsafe. Would be correct only without red flags (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation).

options.text: Anal stenosis; perform serial anal dilatation in the GP clinic
options.rationale: Anal stenosis has a narrow caudal anus on exam; here the anus is normally sited and obstruction is functional from aganglionic segment. Would be correct with anal stenosis on exam (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Cow's milk protein allergy; trial hydrolysed formula and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: CMPA causes loose stools, blood streaks or eczema, not delayed meconium and obstruction. Would be correct with blood-streaked stools and atopy (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Hirschsprung disease; refer for surgical review and arrange suction rectal biopsy
options.rationale: Delayed meconium >48 hours, abdominal distension, faltering growth, ribbon stool with explosive release on PR and bilious vomiting are classic for Hirschsprung disease. Suction rectal biopsy demonstrating aganglionosis is the diagnostic gold standard (RCH CPG; RACS).

options.text: Hypothyroidism; check TSH and free T4 before any further workup
options.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is detected on newborn heelprick and causes prolonged jaundice/lethargy, not delayed meconium with explosive PR. Would be correct with positive Guthrie (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Functional constipation accounts for over 95% of paediatric constipation and is diagnosed clinically using the Rome IV criteria (two or more of: 2 or fewer defecations per week, history of retentive posturing, history of painful or hard bowel movements, faecal mass in the rectum, large stools). Red flags that mandate organic workup include onset under one month of age, delayed meconium passage over 48 hours, ribbon stools, explosive release on rectal exam, faltering growth, bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, abnormal sacrum (dimple, hair tuft, asymmetric gluteal cleft), lower-limb neurology, and severe anal pathology. The leading organic diagnoses are Hirschsprung disease, anorectal malformation, cystic fibrosis (meconium ileus), spinal dysraphism, hypothyroidism and cow's milk protein allergy. Hirschsprung is diagnosed by suction rectal biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and increased acetylcholinesterase-staining nerve fibres; contrast enema shows a transition zone.
key_points: Delayed meconium >48 hours = think Hirschsprung
Explosive release on PR is a classic clue
Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic test
Total colonic Hirschsprung can be associated with Down syndrome
Bilious vomiting in an infant is surgical until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Delayed meconium passage beyond 48 hours, ribbon stools, explosive release on rectal exam, and faltering growth are red flags for Hirschsprung disease - suction rectal biopsy is diagnostic, contrast enema supports surgical planning (RCH CPG Constipation).

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Bilious vomiting

subtopic_detail: Red flag features for organic cause of constipation in an infant

stem:
A 10-month-old boy is reviewed by his GP at the routine immunisation visit. His weight today is 7.6 kg. He was born at 38+5 weighing 3.4 kg (50th centile) and tracked the 50th centile through to 6 months when he weighed 7.5 kg. He is breastfed on demand approximately 4 times a day and was started on solids at 6 months.

His mother reports he is fussy with solids and refuses most foods other than rice porridge. He has no vomiting, no diarrhoeally appropriate. Family height: mother 168 cm, father 180 cm. He has a normal cardiovascular and respiratory examination, no lymphadenopathy, no organomegaly. His length is on the 25th centile and head circumference on the 50th centile. His parents both work full-time and a grandparent provides daytime care; mealtime grazing on biscuits between meals is common.

He has had no antenatal concerns, no NICU admission, and his newborn examination was unremarkable. He has not been screened for coeliac serology, thyroid function or iron studies. His mother breastfeeds approximately 4 times in 24 hours, including overnight, and reports she does not see him take full feeds; he is often distracted at the breast. The mid-parental height projects to the 50th centile.

data_table.Examination: Alert, interactive. No dysmorphism. No oedema. Normal cardiorespiratory exam. No organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Growth chart: weight has dropped from 50th to <10th centile over 4 months; length on 25th; HC on 50th. No investigations done yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial step?

options.text: Start an appetite stimulant such as cyproheptadine and review in one month
options.rationale: Pharmacological appetite stimulation is not first-line in young children; address feeding behaviour, energy density and mealtime environment first. Would be correct only as specialist add-on after multidisciplinary first-line (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth

options.text: Admit to hospital for nasogastric feeding and observe weight gain inpatient
options.rationale: Hospital admission is for severe malnutrition, safeguarding concerns or failed community management. Would be correct with severe undernutrition or social concerns (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth

options.text: Order broad screen: coeliac serology, TFTs, sweat test, metabolic screen now
options.rationale: Investigations are history/exam-guided; only 1-2 percent of faltering growth is non-nutritional organic. Broad untargeted screen is low-yield. Would be correct only with specific red-flag features (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth

options.text: Refer immediately to child protection services on suspicion of parental neglect
options.rationale: Safeguarding is considered if history is inconsistent or other concerns exist; feeding history and dietary advice come first. Would be correct only with specific safeguarding red flags (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth

options.text: Take a detailed feeding history and observe a feed before ordering any investigations
options.rationale: Faltering growth is most commonly due to inadequate intake. The first step is a detailed feeding and dietary history with a feed observation, before targeted investigations are considered. Anthropometry should plot weight, length and head circumference and check for centile crossing (RCH CPG Faltering growth).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth
RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

explanation.summary: Faltering growth is defined by sustained drop across centiles (typically >=2 major centiles on the weight chart). The three buckets are: inadequate intake (commonest), malabsorption, and increased metabolic demand. Always observe a feed and take a 24-hour recall before broad investigation.
key_points: Plot weight, length and head circumference; check for centile crossing
Head sparing in early malnutrition (weight drops, length next, HC last)
Inadequate intake is the commonest cause
Targeted investigations only after history/examination flag a cause
Multidisciplinary management (dietitian, MCHN, paediatrician) for sustained faltering
explanation.pearls: A single low weight is not faltering growth; you need the trajectory. Always plot the chart yourself.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth
RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

subtopic_detail: Assessment of inadequate weight gain in a young child

stem:
An 8-year-old boy is reviewed by a paediatrician at the request of his GP and school. For the last 18 months he has had difficulty sustaining attention to schoolwork, frequently leaves his seat, interrupts conversations, loses his pencil case daily and forgets multi-step instructions. His Year 3 teacher and his swimming coach have completed Conners questionnaires which were both above clinical cut-offs.

His mother reports similar behaviours at home, including weekends and school holidays, since pre-school. He has had no academic regression, has age-appropriate reading and numeracy, has friends but tends to fall out with them over impulsive comments, and has no features of low mood, anxiety, tics, autism or psychosis. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death, long QT or cardiomyopathy.

On examination he is well, height 50th centile, weight 60th centile, BP 100/64, HR 86 with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. There is no history of head injury, no perinatal hypoxia. He has no symptoms of restless legs, no snoring on direct questioning, and his sleep onset latency is appropriate. There is no anxiety disorder, no oppositional defiant features beyond developmental norms, and no learning disability identified on school psychometric assessment.

data_table.Examination: No dysmorphism. Normal cardiovascular and respiratory exam. No tics observed.
data_table.Investigations: Conners ratings above cut-off from two informants. ECG: sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms, no LVH, no pre-excitation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of restricted elimination diet for 6 weeks before pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Restrictive diets are not recommended as primary; behavioural/environmental supports plus stimulants are first-line. Would be correct only with documented food trigger and dietitian oversight (Source: RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Commence atomoxetine as first-line pharmacotherapy without a stimulant trial
options.rationale: Stimulants are first-line in children without contraindications; atomoxetine is for stimulant intolerance, tics, anxiety or diversion risk. Would be correct with those specific indications (Source: AADPA ADHD).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg BD under paediatrician authorisation, behavioural strategies, monitor side effects
options.rationale: He meets DSM-5 ADHD criteria (>=6 months, two settings, functional impairment). Methylphenidate is first-line; in Australia, stimulants require state authority/TGA pathway via a paediatrician. Behavioural interventions run in parallel and monitoring includes growth, BP, HR and mental state (AADPA 2022; RACGP).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Watchful waiting for a further 12 months with a school behavioural plan only
options.rationale: Over 6 months of symptoms with functional impairment in 2 settings warrants treatment; behavioural strategies are necessary but not sufficient. Would be correct only with mild symptoms in one setting (Source: AADPA 2022).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

options.text: Refer for cardiology review and stress echocardiogram before any pharmacotherapy now
options.rationale: Routine cardiology review is not required with unremarkable history/exam/ECG; only red flags (family sudden death, exertional syncope, abnormal ECG) mandate review. Would be correct only with red flags (Source: AADPA).
source_refs: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

explanation.summary: ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptom counts present for >=6 months, in >=2 settings, causing functional impairment, with onset before age 12. First-line pharmacotherapy is a stimulant (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) under paediatrician supervision and state authority/TGA authorisation, alongside behavioural and educational supports.
key_points: DSM-5: >=6 inattentive and/or >=6 hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (<17 yo)
Two settings, >=6 months, onset <12 years, functional impairment
Pre-stimulant: cardiac history, family SCD history, exam, ECG
Monitor height, weight, BP, HR, mood, sleep, appetite
Stimulants are S8 in Australia and require state authority
explanation.pearls: School-only symptoms suggest a learning disorder or environment problem; home-only symptoms suggest parenting or attachment. ADHD is across settings.

sources.label: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

subtopic_detail: Stimulant initiation in school-aged inattention and hyperactivity

stem:
An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his parents for his routine check. His mother has noticed that he does not respond to his name, rarely makes eye contact, does not point to share interest, does not wave, and has only 3 single words used inconsistently. He prefers to line up his cars and becomes distressed when the line is disturbed.

He had normal hearing screening at birth, no birth complications, no significant medical illnesses, and is up to date with immunisations. His gross motor skills are typical (walks well, climbs stairs holding the rail). His paternal cousin has an autism diagnosis. Examination is unremarkable: no dysmorphic features, no neurocutaneous stigmata, no organomegaly. His M-CHAT-R/F at this visit shows a score of 8 (high risk threshold >=8).

There has been no language regression and no loss of previously acquired skills. He had a febrile illness with rash at 11 months that resolved within 48 hours. He attends a long day care 2 days per week and the educators have raised similar concerns to the parents about his lack of response to his name and limited interaction with peers, often playing in parallel rather than reciprocally.

He has had no episodes consistent with seizures, no head injuries and no recurrent ear infections. There is no maternal alcohol use during pregnancy. The parents speak English at home.

data_table.Vital signs: Afebrile, growth on 50th centile across the board.
data_table.Examination: No dysmorphism. No cafe-au-lait macules. No organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: M-CHAT-R/F score 8 (high risk). Newborn hearing screen normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure and review at the 2-year check, as language development is variable
options.rationale: Multiple core ASD features plus M-CHAT-R/F at high-risk threshold; reassurance delays access to early intervention. Would be correct only with isolated mild language delay and normal social communication (Source: Autism CRC National Guideline).
source_refs: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

options.text: Refer to multidisciplinary autism assessment, arrange audiology, lodge NDIS Early Childhood referral
options.rationale: M-CHAT-R/F >=8 with concerning features warrants referral for a multidisciplinary autism assessment, formal audiology to exclude hearing loss as a contributor, and early access to NDIS Early Childhood early intervention (available before formal diagnosis if developmental delay is documented) (Autism CRC National Guideline; NDIS Early Childhood).
source_refs: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

options.text: Order chromosomal microarray and Fragile X testing today, review when results return
options.rationale: Genetic testing (CMA, Fragile X) is arranged by the paediatrician alongside multidisciplinary assessment, not as a standalone first step. Would be correct as part of paediatrician-led workup (Source: Autism CRC).
source_refs: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

options.text: Commence risperidone now for irritability and stereotyped repetitive behavioural features
options.rationale: ASD pharmacotherapy is reserved for specific severe behavioural symptoms after specialist assessment; not first-line at age 18 months. Would be correct only after specialist review for severe behaviour (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

options.text: Refer only to speech pathology and review again in 6 months
options.rationale: Speech pathology alone misses multidimensional ASD; multidisciplinary assessment with audiology and NDIS early intervention is needed. Would be correct only as adjunct, not sole referral (Source: Autism CRC).
source_refs: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

explanation.summary: ASD diagnostic criteria require persistent deficits in social communication AND restricted/repetitive behaviours, present from early development, causing functional impairment. M-CHAT-R/F is the validated 18-24 month screen. NDIS Early Childhood provides early intervention before formal diagnosis if developmental delay is documented.
key_points: Red flags at 18 mo: no response to name, no joint attention, no pointing to share, language regression
M-CHAT-R/F: low risk 0-2, medium 3-7 (do follow-up), high >=8 (refer)
Always check hearing in any speech delay
Multidisciplinary assessment: paediatrician + speech path + psychology
NDIS Early Childhood is available from before diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Joint attention (sharing interest with another person via gaze or pointing) is the single most predictive early autism feature.

sources.label: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

subtopic_detail: Developmental concern at 18 months with social communication features

stem:
A 15-year-old girl presents to her GP with her mother, who has found superficial linear cuts on her daughter's forearm. The patient initially declines to speak in front of her mother, so the GP sees her alone after confidentiality is explained. Over a confidential HEEADSSS interview she describes 4 months of low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, poor concentration affecting her grades, weight loss of 3 kg, and feelings of hopelessness.

She has been cutting her forearm with a razor weekly for 6 weeks for emotional relief. She reports passive thoughts that she would rather not be alive, but denies a current plan, intent, access to means, or previous attempts. There is no substance use, she is sexually inexperienced, and there is no disclosure of abuse. Her older brother has bipolar disorder.

She agrees that her mother can be involved in safety planning. Examination shows superficial linear forearm cuts, no active bleeding, no concerning weight loss on growth chart, BMI 19. There is no history of trauma, sexual abuse or domestic violence on confidential enquiry, and no eating disorder behaviours (no restriction, no binge-purge). She agrees to a follow-up plan and accepts referral to local youth mental health services.

data_table.Examination: Multiple superficial linear forearm cuts. Affect flat. Speech soft but coherent. No psychotic features. No infection.
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated at the initial visit.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Maintain confidentiality from the parents in all circumstances given she is a Gillick-competent minor
options.rationale: Confidentiality has limits with risk of harm; involving a supportive parent with consent improves outcomes. Would be correct only if there were no immediate safety concern (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality).

options.text: Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily today with no psychological referral, and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate adolescent depression; fluoxetine starts at 10 mg with close monitoring alongside therapy. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Admit involuntarily to a secure adolescent inpatient mental health unit today
options.rationale: Involuntary admission is for acute risk with intent/plan or inability to maintain safety. Passive ideation without plan, with family support, and willingness to engage warrants community management. Would be correct only with imminent risk and refusal to engage (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Written safety plan (means restriction, supports), involve mother, GP within 1 week, refer CAMHS
options.rationale: Adolescent depression with self-harm and passive suicidal ideation requires a documented safety plan with means restriction, family involvement (with consent where possible), early review, and specialist mental health referral. headspace and CAMHS provide tiered care. Pharmacotherapy is considered when psychological therapy is insufficient or symptoms are moderate-severe and persistent (RANZCP; headspace).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

options.text: Reassure that self-harm without suicidal intent is normal adolescence and review 3 months
options.rationale: Self-harm with persistent low mood and passive suicidal ideation is a mental illness marker and elevated future suicide risk. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP; headspace).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

explanation.summary: Adolescent depression with self-harm requires a HEEADSSS-based assessment, structured suicide risk assessment, a written safety plan, family involvement with consent, and tiered referral (GP -> headspace -> CAMHS). Fluoxetine is the first-line SSRI for adolescents with moderate-severe depression, started low with early review for activation.
key_points: HEEADSSS: Home, Education/Employment, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/self-harm, Safety
Safety plan: warning signs, coping, distractions, social supports, professional contacts, means restriction
Fluoxetine is the SSRI with the best evidence in adolescent depression
Black-box: monitor closely for activation/suicidality first 4 weeks of SSRI
headspace is the national youth mental health service (12-25 yo)
explanation.pearls: Means restriction (lock medicines, remove blades) is the single highest-impact element of a safety plan.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

stem:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to ED by her parents after collapsing on standing. Over 9 months her weight has dropped from 58 kg to 38 kg; her height is 165 cm (BMI 14.0). She restricts intake to <600 kcal/day, exercises for 2-3 hours daily and induces vomiting after any meal she perceives as 'too much'. She has not menstruated for 5 months.

She insists she is fat. There has been no binge eating, no laxative use, no diuretic use, no substance use, no overt depressive symptoms beyond food preoccupation, no suicidal ideation. Examination: alert, cooperative, dry mucous membranes, lanugo on the back, BP 84/52 supine dropping to 70/40 on standing with HR 38 rising to 64, T 35.4 degC, capillary refill 3 sec, peripherally cool.

ECG shows sinus bradycardia, QTc 470 ms. Bedside investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, phosphate 0.7, magnesium 0.6, glucose 3.4, urea 6.2, creatinine 64, ALT 110. She denies laxative or diuretic use. She has no chest pain, no palpitations on exertion, no syncope episodes other than this collapse. She has no haematemesis, no diarrhoea, no constipation other than infrequent stool, and no abdominal pain. Family history: maternal aunt with bulimia nervosa, no thyroid disease, no diabetes, no coeliac disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/52 supine -> 70/40 standing, HR 38 -> 64 standing, T 35.4 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 14.0. Lanugo. Cool peripheries. Capillary refill 3 sec. No focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, PO4 0.7 mmol/L, Mg 0.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, ALT 110 U/L. ECG: sinus bradycardia 38, QTc 470 ms.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient paediatric dietitian and weekly community GP review
options.rationale: Multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria mandate inpatient admission. Would be correct only with low-risk vitals and electrolytes (Source: RANZCP; ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nightly for body image distortion and discharge home
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive in eating disorders, not a substitute for medical stabilisation and family-based treatment. Would be correct as adjunct after stabilisation (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Admit and commence high-calorie refeeding at 2000 kcal/day from day 1 to rapidly restore weight
options.rationale: Aggressive refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome (fluid overload, hypophosphataemia, arrhythmia, Wernicke encephalopathy). Would never be correct in high-risk patient (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa

options.text: Admit to psychiatric ward and defer medical management until psychiatric review tomorrow
options.rationale: She is medically unstable; medical stabilisation precedes or runs alongside psychiatric admission. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Admit medical bed; cautious refeeding (~5-10 kcal/kg/day), daily electrolyte monitoring, prophylactic phosphate/thiamine, refer mental health
options.rationale: She has severe anorexia nervosa with refeeding syndrome risk (low PO4, low Mg, low K, low glucose). MARSIPAN-AU mandates medical admission with cautious refeeding, electrolyte and glucose monitoring, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia, and FBT engagement (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: MARSIPAN-AU high-risk indicators for anorexia nervosa admission include BMI <14 (or <13 in adolescents), HR <45, postural BP fall >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc >450 ms, hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypoglycaemia and acute medical instability. Refeeding starts low (5-10 kcal/kg/day) with daily electrolytes, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring.
key_points: Refeeding syndrome: low PO4, K, Mg; fluid shifts; arrhythmia; Wernicke
Start refeeding cautiously - 'start low, go slow' especially in severe AN
Prophylactic thiamine 100 mg before any glucose-containing fluids
Family-Based Treatment (FBT/Maudsley) is first-line psychotherapy for adolescent AN
Monitor QTc daily during refeeding
explanation.pearls: The first 72 hours of refeeding kill people, not the starvation. Phosphate is the canary.

sources.label: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

subtopic_detail: Severely underweight adolescent with food restriction

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is reviewed two weeks after her second culture-proven E coli febrile UTI (fever to 39 deg C) treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole per local sensitivities. The first UTI was 4 months ago. She has ongoing functional constipation well controlled with macrogol. She is otherwise well, growth on the 60th centile, with normal developmental milestones and an unremarkable antenatal renal ultrasound.

There is no family history of renal disease or VUR. Renal ultrasound after the recent admission shows mildly dilated left renal pelvis (AP diameter 8 mm), no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex system, and normal bladder wall with no post-void residual. The GP is planning the next investigation.

data_table.Examination: Well, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Renal USS: left renal pelvis AP 8 mm, no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex. Urine MCS clear since treatment.

lead_in: Which next investigation is most appropriate, and when should it be performed?

options.text: DMSA scan 4-6 months after the most recent UTI to detect cortical scarring; reserve MCUG for hydroureter, abnormal DMSA or breakthrough infections
options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI and KHA-CARI: after recurrent febrile UTI in a young child, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection detects persistent scarring (acute inflammation has settled by then). MCUG is invasive and is reserved for hydroureter, abnormal DMSA or breakthrough disease, not first-line. Concurrent constipation management is essential.

options.text: MCUG within the next week as the next investigation, with prophylactic trimethoprim continued until the result is reviewed in clinic
options.rationale: MCUG (catheter, ionising radiation) is not first-line after recurrent UTI in AU pathway; DMSA is preferred to stratify further imaging. Would be correct with hydroureter, abnormal voiding or DMSA-detected scarring (Source: KHA-CARI; RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence trimethoprim nightly as long-term prophylaxis without any further imaging to delineate cortical scarring or vesicoureteric reflux pattern
options.rationale: Prophylaxis is considered after risk stratification (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); empirical prophylaxis without imaging is not first-line. Would be correct after DMSA shows significant scarring or high-grade VUR (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Refer for urological cystoscopy to evaluate the bladder mucosa for inflammation or anatomical abnormality before any other further imaging investigation
options.rationale: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on US. Would be correct with bladder anomaly on US (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Repeat renal ultrasound in 12 months and otherwise observe without DMSA or MCUG, with watchful waiting and parental return advice for fever
options.rationale: After two febrile UTIs, deferred imaging misses scarring; DMSA at 4-6 months is recommended. Would be correct with single UTI and resolved clinical picture (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: After recurrent febrile UTI in young children, RCH and KHA-CARI recommend renal ultrasound acutely and DMSA 4-6 months later to assess for cortical scarring. MCUG is targeted (abnormal USS, abnormal DMSA, or further breakthroughs). Constipation contributes substantially to UTI recurrence and must be optimised.
key_points: USS during/after first febrile UTI in any child <2 yo
DMSA 4-6 months after recurrent febrile UTI
MCUG if hydroureter, dilated collecting system, or further breakthrough
Treat constipation - reduces UTI recurrence
Prophylaxis individualised: high-grade VUR, severe scarring, breakthrough UTI
explanation.pearls: If you can fix the constipation you fix the UTI in many of these children.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Urinary tract infection

subtopic_detail: Imaging after a second febrile UTI in a young girl

stem:
A 17-month-old boy attends his GP for delayed 12-month immunisations because the family has been overseas. He is well, immunocompetent, with no chronic illness. His mother is on the bone marrow transplant register and has just commenced cyclophosphamide-based conditioning for an upcoming allogeneic transplant in 4 weeks; she lives in the same household. The boy's 4-year-old sister is also up to date.

His mother is concerned about the safety of his vaccines given her own state and asks the GP for advice. His outstanding 12-month vaccines include MMR, meningococcal ACWY and the third dose of 13vPCV. He has had no recent live vaccines, no immunosuppressive therapy of his own, and no contact with active tuberculosis. The mother's transplant team has been in contact with the family GP and a written summary of household infection-control measures is being prepared.

The mother has had her own pre-transplant influenza, pneumococcal and any catch-up inactivated vaccines administered as advised by haematology. The father is asymptomatic and fully vaccinated.

data_table.Examination: Well child, no rash, no respiratory or other illness.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding his catch-up immunisations?

options.text: Defer all of the child's vaccines until after the mother's transplant and engraftment
options.rationale: Deferring all vaccines leaves the immunocompromised mother more vulnerable to vaccine-preventable disease in the home; child should be fully immunised, including most live vaccines. Would never be correct as blanket policy (Source: NCIRS).

options.text: Give MMR and varicella as scheduled; routine isolation not required (manage rashes with aciclovir)
options.rationale: MMR and varicella vaccine viruses are not transmitted from immunocompetent vaccinees in routine contact (varicella vaccine virus transmission is rare and only from vesicular rash). Immunising household contacts of immunocompromised people is recommended. Rotavirus is the exception (faecal shedding) and would need hand hygiene precautions; he is too old for rotavirus anyway (NCIRS).

options.text: Replace MMR with an inactivated alternative because of household immunocompromise concerns
options.rationale: No inactivated MMR alternative exists on NIP; MMR vaccine viruses are not transmitted from vaccinees to contacts. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).

options.text: Give the child oral typhoid (live) vaccine prophylactically because of expected hospital visits
options.rationale: Oral typhoid is not routinely indicated and not part of NIP catch-up; introduces a live oral vaccine inappropriately. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).

options.text: Give only inactivated vaccines and avoid live vaccines until mother is post-transplant immune-reconstituted
options.rationale: MMR and varicella are NOT contraindicated in household contacts of immunocompromised people; he remains at risk if unprotected. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS; eviQ).

explanation.summary: Live vaccines are contraindicated IN the immunocompromised person but in general are SAFE for their household contacts (MMR not transmitted; varicella vaccine virus transmission rare and only via vesicular rash). Rotavirus is the practical exception due to faecal shedding - hand hygiene needed for 4-6 weeks. Influenza inactivated is encouraged for household contacts annually.
key_points: Live vaccines contraindicated IN immunocompromised individuals
Household contacts SHOULD be immunised, including MMR and varicella
Manage post-varicella rash with covering/isolation
Rotavirus faecal shedding - hand hygiene precaution for 4-6 weeks
Influenza (inactivated) annually for all household contacts
explanation.pearls: Cocooning protects the immunocompromised; under-immunising the household is the opposite of safe.

subtopic_detail: Live vaccine considerations in a household with immunocompromise

stem:
A 5-month-old breastfed boy attends his GP with his father, who has read 'a lot online' and wants to delay the 6-month vaccines. The mother is supportive of vaccination and has asked the father to discuss it with the doctor. The father is hesitant rather than refusing; his main concerns are that 'too many vaccines at once overload the immune system' and that aluminium adjuvants 'cause autism'.

The infant is otherwise well, growing on the 50th centile, with no medical issues. He had his 2 and 4-month vaccines without adverse events. There is no household medical neglect and the parents otherwise engage with primary care.
lead_in: Which approach is most consistent with current Australian NCIRS SKAI and RACGP guidance for vaccine hesitancy?

options.text: Refuse to provide further GP care to the family until the father agrees to the standard immunisation schedule for both of his children
options.rationale: Withholding general care is unethical and not supported by RACGP/NCIRS; trusted continuity is a key driver of eventual acceptance. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; NCIRS SKAI).

options.text: Motivational-interviewing-style consultation - presumptive language, explore concerns, share tailored evidence on immune-overload and aluminium myths, offer SKAI resources
options.rationale: NCIRS SKAI and RACGP recommend MI-style engagement: presumptive language, exploration of concerns, tailored factual rebuttal of specific myths, a clear clinician recommendation and signposting to reputable resources, with a planned follow-up. Adversarial debate and alternative schedules are less effective.

options.text: Notify child protection because vaccine refusal constitutes medical neglect under mandatory reporting obligations for treating clinicians in this family
options.rationale: Vaccine hesitancy in an otherwise well-cared-for child is not itself medical neglect. Would be correct only with broader documented neglect (Source: NCIRS).

options.text: Offer a spaced-out alternative schedule giving only one vaccine per visit to accommodate parental concerns about immune overload from co-administration
options.rationale: Alternative schedules prolong susceptibility, increase visits/pain, and reinforce immune-overload misconception. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS SKAI).

options.text: Refer to the Family Court to compel vaccination over parental objection
options.rationale: Court orders are a last resort after parental deadlock and other approaches have failed; initial step is shared GP-led discussion. Would be correct only after exhausted negotiation and ongoing risk to child (Source: NCIRS).

explanation.summary: Vaccine hesitancy is best addressed with a presumptive opening, motivational interviewing, exploration of specific concerns, tailored factual rebuttal, an explicit clinician recommendation, and signposting to evidence-based resources (NCIRS SKAI). Maintain the therapeutic relationship; many hesitant parents accept vaccines over time with continuity.
key_points: Presumptive opening: 'Today we'll do the 6-month vaccines.'
Motivational interviewing: explore, affirm, reflect, summarise
Address specific concerns with specific evidence
Make an explicit recommendation - 'I recommend...'
SKAI website is the Australian go-to resource
explanation.pearls: Don't try to win the argument in one visit; you are building a relationship that often takes 2-3 conversations to land.

subtopic_detail: Parental hesitancy about routine childhood vaccination

stem:
An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his mother because of bow legs and reluctance to walk. He was born at term, exclusively breastfed until 12 months without vitamin D supplementation, and has had limited sun exposure as the family wear concealing clothing for cultural reasons and live in a southern Australian city. His diet is otherwise low in dairy.

There is no history of diarrhoea, fatty stools, recurrent infections, fractures or a family history of metabolic bone disease. He is on the 25th centile for length and 50th for weight. On examination he has frontal bossing, widening of the wrists, palpable rachitic rosary at the costochondral junctions, and genu varum. He has no carpopedal spasm and no seizure activity.

His mother is otherwise well but reports myalgia and fatigue. The infant takes solids three times a day, including iron-fortified cereal, but very little dairy or oily fish. There is no family history of consanguinity, fragility fractures, or genetic bone disease. He has met expected developmental milestones for fine motor and language but has been slower to walk; he took his first independent steps at 16 months and is now unsteady, with frequent falls noticed by his mother. The wrist X-ray was ordered after the GP examined him today and the radiologist phoned through the metaphyseal findings.

data_table.Examination: Frontal bossing, wrist widening, rachitic rosary, genu varum. No tetany. Length 25th centile, weight 50th.
data_table.Investigations: 25-OH vitamin D 18 nmol/L, calcium 2.10 mmol/L (corrected), phosphate 0.9 mmol/L, ALP 920 U/L (markedly elevated), PTH 14.2 pmol/L (high), creatinine 24 micromol/L. Wrist X-ray: cupping and fraying of the distal radial and ulnar metaphyses.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Bisphosphonates orally to correct the bony lower limb deformity
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates have no role in nutritional rickets and may impair mineralisation; deformity usually corrects with vitamin D and calcium repletion. Would never be correct in nutritional rickets (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency

options.text: A single high-dose 'stoss' of cholecalciferol 600,000 IU orally with no follow-up
options.rationale: Stoss therapy carries hypercalcaemia risk in young children and limits follow-up biochemistry; daily replacement is safer and standard in Australia. Would be correct only in selected older children with adherence concerns under specialist input (Source: APEG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency

options.text: IV calcium gluconate today and admit for cardiac telemetry monitoring
options.rationale: IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, ECG changes); he is asymptomatic. Would be correct with tetany, seizures or ECG changes (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency

options.text: Calcitriol (1,25-OH vitamin D) 0.25 microg daily orally as first-line
options.rationale: Calcitriol is reserved for vitamin D-dependent rickets, CKD and hypoparathyroidism; nutritional deficiency uses cholecalciferol because 1-alpha-hydroxylation is intact. Would be correct in CKD or VDDR (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency

options.text: Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months, optimise calcium, screen mother
options.rationale: Confirmed nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets (low 25-OH vitamin D <50 nmol/L, raised ALP and PTH, radiographic changes) is treated with cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months in a toddler, plus 500 mg/day elemental calcium, with sun exposure counselling. Screen the breastfeeding mother because deficient mothers explain the infant's deficiency (RCH CPG Vitamin D; APEG/ASBMR consensus).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency
APEG/ASBMR Australian and New Zealand consensus on vitamin D

explanation.summary: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets is treated with cholecalciferol (1000-2000 IU/day for toddlers; 600 IU/day prevention) for 3 months, with calcium intake optimisation and sun exposure counselling. Always screen the breastfeeding mother. The 25-OH vitamin D threshold for deficiency is <50 nmol/L (severe <30 nmol/L).
key_points: 25-OH vitamin D: <30 severe deficient, <50 deficient, 50-75 insufficient
Raised ALP and PTH are early biochemical markers
X-ray: cupping and fraying of metaphyses (wrist, knees, ankles)
Treat with cholecalciferol (daily) - not calcitriol - for nutritional rickets
Screen the mother; vitamin D-deficient mothers explain vitamin D-deficient infants
explanation.pearls: If ALP is 'unbelievably high' in a toddler, think rickets before liver. ALP is the most sensitive biochemical sign.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency
APEG/ASBMR Australian and New Zealand consensus on vitamin D

subtopic_detail: Toddler with bow legs and biochemistry suggestive of metabolic bone disease
